content.text: What it is
Quality signals are the AI-specific observability metrics that tell you whether an agentic system is producing
correct, safe, and useful

outputs — independent of whether it is running fast or slow. They sit alongside the four classic golden signals (latency, traffic, errors, saturation) and answer a different question: not "is the system up?" but "is the system right?"

Eval drift is the phenomenon where these quality scores change over time without an explicit code deployment. A model provider update, a subtle shift in the user input distribution, a prompt engineering tweak, or a change in retrieved context can all push quality metrics away from the baseline you established at launch.
Why it matters

Infrastructure metrics alone cannot detect quality regressions in AI systems. A model that starts hallucinating facts, refusing valid requests, or producing incoherent outputs will still show normal latency and error rates. Without quality monitoring, regressions go undetected until users notice and complain — often days or weeks after the degradation began.

Eval drift is especially insidious because it can be gradual. A week-over-week decline of 0.5 points on a correctness metric is invisible in a single dashboard snapshot but compounds to a meaningful degradation over a quarter.
Benefits
Early detection of silent regressions before users file support tickets.
Objective accountability for prompt changes, model upgrades, and retrieval pipeline modifications.
Measurable quality SLOs that align engineering effort with user-facing outcomes.
Rollback triggers grounded in data rather than gut instinct.
Continuous evidence for model governance, compliance, and audit trails.
Trade-offs
LLM-as-judge evaluations add latency and cost to every sample evaluated. Running evals on 100% of traffic is rarely economical.
Reference-based metrics (ROUGE, BLEU, semantic similarity) require ground-truth answers, which are expensive to maintain.
Drift detection thresholds are domain-specific. A universal alert threshold does not exist — you must calibrate per task type.
Human feedback signals (thumbs up/down) are sparse and biased; they supplement but cannot replace automated evals.
How it works

The system continuously or periodically samples agent inputs and outputs, runs them through an eval pipeline, and writes scored records to a timeseries store. A drift detector compares the current rolling window of scores to a frozen baseline computed at a known-good version of the system. When the gap exceeds a threshold, an alert fires.
Key eval dimensions for agentic AI:
Task completion rate — did the agent finish the goal it was given?
Groundedness — does the output trace to the provided context (no hallucination)?
Refusal rate — is the model declining valid requests more or less often than expected?
Semantic similarity — how close is the output embedding to a reference answer?
Output coherence — is the response well-structured and self-consistent?
Human feedback score — aggregated thumbs-up / thumbs-down or star ratings.
Architecture
The diagram below shows the eval drift monitoring pipeline from agent output through to alerting.

attrs.alt: Eval drift monitoring pipeline: Agent Output to Eval Runner to Score Timeseries DB to Drift Detector to Alert, with Baseline Store as reference

content.text: Core components:
Sampler — selects a fraction of live traffic (or all of it for lower-volume systems) to feed into the eval pipeline.
Eval runner — applies one or more scorers (LLM-as-judge, embedding similarity, rule-based classifiers) to each sample.
Score timeseries store — persists {timestamp, metric_name, score, metadata} rows. Prometheus remote-write or a columnar store like ClickHouse both work.
Baseline store — a snapshot of expected score distributions taken at a tagged release.
Drift detector — computes a rolling window statistic and compares it to the baseline, emitting alerts when delta exceeds a threshold.
When to use it
Any production agentic system where output quality is load-bearing — customer support, code generation, document review.
When the underlying model is managed by a third-party provider and may be updated without your control.
When prompt engineering is ongoing — you need to prove that each prompt change does not regress existing capabilities.
After a RAG pipeline modification — retrieved context quality directly affects groundedness scores.
How to implement
Implementation has three phases: establishing the baseline, running continuous evals, and wiring up drift detection.

Phase 1 — Establish a baseline. At a known-good release, run your eval suite against a representative sample of real inputs and store the resulting score distribution (mean and standard deviation per metric). Tag this snapshot with the release version.

Phase 2 — Choose eval cadence. Options range from evaluating every request (expensive, gives real-time signal) to periodic batch jobs (cheaper, latency in detection). A common pattern is 1–5% online sampling plus a nightly batch eval against a fixed benchmark dataset.
Phase 3 — Set alerting thresholds. A z-score of ±2 on a rolling 7-day window is a reasonable starting point. Tune based on your baseline variance — high-variance metrics need wider bands.
Examples
The following Python snippet implements a minimal z-score drift detector that reads scores from a store and fires an alert when drift is detected.

import statistics
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import List


@dataclass
class Baseline:
    metric: str
    mean: float
    std: float


def compute_baseline(scores: List[float]) -> tuple[float, float]:
    """Return (mean, std) from a list of historical scores."""
    mean = statistics.mean(scores)
    std = statistics.stdev(scores)
    return mean, std


def detect_drift(
    recent_scores: List[float],
    baseline: Baseline,
    threshold_z: float = 2.0,
) -> dict:
    """Compare a recent window of scores to a baseline.

    Returns a dict with 'drifted' bool and 'z_score' float.
    """
    if not recent_scores:
        return {"drifted": False, "z_score": 0.0}

    window_mean = statistics.mean(recent_scores)

    if baseline.std == 0:
        # Perfectly stable baseline; any deviation counts as drift.
        z_score = float("inf") if window_mean != baseline.mean else 0.0
    else:
        z_score = (window_mean - baseline.mean) / baseline.std

    drifted = abs(z_score) > threshold_z
    return {
        "metric": baseline.metric,
        "window_mean": window_mean,
        "baseline_mean": baseline.mean,
        "z_score": round(z_score, 3),
        "drifted": drifted,
    }


# --- Example usage ---
historical_groundedness = [0.87, 0.85, 0.88, 0.86, 0.89, 0.84, 0.87]
mean, std = compute_baseline(historical_groundedness)
baseline = Baseline(metric="groundedness", mean=mean, std=std)

# Recent window after a model update
recent = [0.74, 0.71, 0.73, 0.70, 0.75]
result = detect_drift(recent, baseline, threshold_z=2.0)
print(result)
# {'metric': 'groundedness', 'window_mean': 0.726, 'baseline_mean': 0.865,
#  'z_score': -4.37, 'drifted': True}
A simple LLM-as-judge scorer using the Claude API:

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()


def score_groundedness(context: str, answer: str) -> float:
    """Ask Claude to rate how grounded the answer is in the context.

    Returns a float in [0.0, 1.0].
    """
    prompt = (
        f"Context:\n{context}\n\n"
        f"Answer:\n{answer}\n\n"
        "Rate how well the answer is grounded in the context. "
        "Respond with only a single number from 0 to 10, where 10 means "
        "every claim in the answer is directly supported by the context."
    )
    message = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=8,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    raw = message.content[0].text.strip()
    try:
        return float(raw) / 10.0
    except ValueError:
        return 0.0
Real-world example
A legal document review agent has a groundedness baseline of 0.88 ± 0.03 established at v1.2 of the system. The team uses 2% online sampling to run LLM-as-judge groundedness scoring on live traffic.

When the upstream model provider silently updates their API with a fine-tuned version, the rolling 7-day groundedness mean drops to 0.76. The drift detector fires a z-score alert of −4.0. The team compares the current model outputs to the baseline batch, identifies a pattern of unsupported legal citations, and rolls back to the pinned model version within two hours of the alert — before any user complaints.
Common mistakes
Setting the baseline on a training or staging dataset instead of representative production inputs. The baseline must reflect real user behavior.
Using a single aggregate score per metric and ignoring per-task-type breakdowns. A model may degrade on code tasks while improving on summarization — the aggregate masks this.
Treating eval drift alerts as high-urgency pages before establishing that the signal is reliable. Start with Slack notifications and build confidence before wiring to PagerDuty.
Never updating the baseline after intentional quality improvements. Baselines must be refreshed whenever you ship a deliberate upgrade.
Using LLM-as-judge with the same model you are monitoring. An updated model may grade its own outputs differently, inflating or deflating scores artificially.
Best practices
Maintain a frozen golden benchmark dataset (100–500 examples with expected answers) and run nightly batch evals against it. Online sampling complements but does not replace this.
Tag every baseline snapshot with the model version, prompt version, and retrieval config hash so drift can be attributed to a specific change.
Use a separate, pinned judge model for LLM-as-judge scoring to ensure the scoring function itself is stable.
Segment scores by user cohort, task type, and input length. Drift in one segment is easier to diagnose than drift in an undifferentiated average.
Publish quality metrics to the same dashboards as infrastructure metrics so on-call engineers see them alongside latency and error rate.
Performance and scalability
LLM-as-judge evaluations can cost as much as the original inference if run at 100% sampling. Practical approaches to keep eval cost bounded:
Sample 1–5% of live traffic for online eval; use a smaller, cheaper judge model (e.g., claude-haiku-4-5) for fast scores and escalate to a larger judge only on suspicious samples.
Batch nightly evals using prompt caching to reuse the system prompt across all benchmark examples — this typically cuts judge costs by 60–80%.
Use rule-based and embedding-based scorers (fast, cheap) as first-pass filters; escalate only borderline samples to LLM judges.
Statistical process control allows you to detect drift with as few as 20–30 samples per window if variance is low, minimizing the eval call count.
Tooling and ecosystem
Several frameworks and platforms provide eval infrastructure for agentic AI:
Braintrust — managed eval platform with baseline comparison, LLM judges, and Prometheus metrics export.
Langfuse — open-source LLM observability with scoring API, dataset management, and Grafana integration.
Inspect AI (UK AISI) — open-source eval framework well-suited to agentic task evaluation with multi-step tracing.
Prometheus + Grafana — the natural timeseries backend for eval scores, alerting on rolling window degradation.
ClickHouse — columnar store suited to storing and querying millions of per-sample score records efficiently.
Comparison with alternatives
The table below compares the main drift detection methods available for AI quality monitoring.
Method
Sensitivity
Sample size needed
False positive risk
Best for
Z-score on rolling mean
Medium
Low (tunable)
General purpose, most agentic systems
CUSUM (cumulative sum)
High
Medium
Gradual slow drift detection
Mann-Whitney U test
Medium-High
Low
Non-normal score distributions
Threshold on absolute score
Low
High
Hard safety floors (e.g., refusal rate > 50%)
Security and reliability
Eval samples may contain sensitive user data. Ensure the eval pipeline applies the same data governance controls (encryption, access logging, retention limits) as the production system.

The judge model is a dependency of your monitoring system. If it is unavailable, alerts go dark. Build circuit breakers that fall back to rule-based scoring and page on-call when the eval pipeline itself is down.

Adversarial inputs can manipulate LLM-as-judge scores. Run input sanitization before samples enter the eval pipeline, or use judge prompts that explicitly instruct the judge to ignore injection attempts.
Maintain immutability of baseline snapshots. Store them in version-controlled object storage with object lock enabled so baselines cannot be retroactively altered.
Mental model
Think of quality signals and eval drift monitoring as the equivalent of
statistical process control

from manufacturing applied to AI output. A factory that makes precision parts does not wait for customers to return defective units — it samples the production line continuously, compares measurements to a tolerance window, and halts the line when variance exceeds specification.

Eval drift monitoring applies the same discipline to model outputs. The baseline is the specification. The rolling eval window is the sample measurement. The z-score alert is the out-of-tolerance signal. The remediation — rollback, prompt revision, model pin — is stopping the line and fixing the root cause.
Key takeaways
Quality signals are AI-specific golden signals that measure correctness, safety, and usefulness — not just infrastructure health.
Eval drift occurs when quality metrics shift over time due to model updates, prompt changes, or input distribution shifts.
A baseline snapshot at a known-good release is the anchor for all drift detection; keep it versioned and immutable.
Z-score on a rolling window is the simplest practical drift detector; CUSUM is better for slow gradual degradation.
Use a pinned, separate judge model for LLM-as-judge scoring to keep the evaluation function stable.
Balance eval coverage against cost: 1–5% online sampling plus nightly batch evals covers most production systems affordably.
